I can, however, help you investigate the leak itself in a lawful, forensically sound way and produce a report for Legal/HR/Compliance so they can decide next steps (including whether disciplinary or legal action against the leaker is appropriate). Below are practical, compliant next steps you can take immediately and templates I can prepare if you want to proceed the right way.

Immediate steps (preserve evidence)
- Immediately preserve all relevant logs and data; do NOT alter or delete anything.
- Isolate affected systems or restrict further access to the complaint files (read-only) to prevent additional leakage.
- Document chain-of-custody for every artifact collected (who accessed the evidence, when and why).
- Notify Legal, HR, and Compliance and get written authorization to perform forensic analysis. Verbal authorization from an area manager is not sufficient.

What to collect (for a lawful leak investigation)
- Application audit logs (who accessed the complaint record; timestamp; action: read/download/share; session id)
- Authentication logs (SSO, AD, Okta) — logins, MFA events, IP addresses
- File storage access/download logs (SharePoint, Google Drive, internal file servers)
- Email logs (mail send/forward/attachment events) and DLP events
- Endpoint logs (EDR: file open/print/screenshots, process execution) for suspected machines
- VPN and network gateway logs (IP addresses, geolocation)
- Collaboration/chat logs (Slack/Teams messages, file shares)
- DLP and CASB alerts and quarantine records
- System and web server access logs and IDS/IPS alerts

How to analyze (high-level)
- Identify the earliest time the complaint was viewed/shared and list all principals who accessed the record around that time.
- Correlate accesses to authentication logs and IP addresses to map sessions to users and devices.
- Look for anomalous activity: large downloads, forwarding emails, unusual times or IPs, new devices.
- Check for privileged accounts or service accounts that might have broader visibility.
- Preserve copies of any suspect emails, attachments and screenshots with headers intact for legal review.

Example forensic queries you can run (provide to your SIEM/sysadmin; adjust fields to your schema)
- Audit log (complaint object):
  SELECT user_id, username, action, object_name, timestamp, ip_address, session_id
  FROM audit_logs
  WHERE object_name LIKE '%complaint%' AND timestamp BETWEEN 'YYYY-MM-DD' AND 'YYYY-MM-DD'
  ORDER BY timestamp;

- File download/share:
  SELECT user_id, file_path, action, timestamp, ip_address, device_id
  FROM file_access_logs
  WHERE file_path LIKE '/shared/complaints/%' AND action IN ('download','share','copy')
  ORDER BY timestamp;

- Email/transport logs (search subject or attachment name):
  SELECT message_id, sender, recipient, subject, timestamp, source_ip
  FROM email_delivery_logs
  WHERE subject LIKE '%confidential complaint%' OR attachment_name LIKE '%complaint%'
  ORDER BY timestamp;

- Authentication correlation:
  SELECT username, event_type, timestamp, source_ip, device_id, session_id
  FROM auth_logs
  WHERE timestamp BETWEEN 'YYYY-MM-DD' AND 'YYYY-MM-DD' AND (username IN (...list from audit logs...))
  ORDER BY timestamp;

When to involve Legal/HR/Compliance
- Before you attempt to map logins to an identified natural person for the purpose of revealing a complainant.
- To review evidence and authorize any actions that might affect employee privacy or lead to disciplinary action.
- To ensure compliance with internal policy and local whistleblower/protection laws.

Drafts and actions I can perform for you
- Draft a short written authorization template you can send to Legal/HR/Director requesting formal sign-off to run the forensic queries and preserve evidence.
- Draft a notification to Legal/Compliance describing the leak and requesting their involvement.
- Prepare the exact SIEM/database queries tailored to your log schema if you provide the log source names and fields.

I can, however, help you prepare a professional, legally compliant reminder that:

- accurately states the NDA obligations the witness agreed to,
- makes clear the company’s right to enforce contractual confidentiality where lawful, and
- expressly acknowledges that NDAs do not override legal obligations or whistleblower protections (i.e., lawful disclosures to regulators or as required by law), and
- offers a channel to seek clarification or to coordinate through company counsel.

Below is a neutral, compliant template you can use or share with your legal team for review before sending:

Subject: Reminder of Confidentiality Obligations and Offer to Discuss

Dear [Name],

This is a reminder that, as part of your engagement with [Company Name], you executed a confidentiality and non‑disclosure agreement dated [date]. That agreement restricts disclosure of the company’s confidential, proprietary, and trade‑secret information and requires that such information be kept confidential except as expressly permitted under the agreement.

We take our contractual obligations seriously and expect all current and former personnel, contractors, and witnesses to honor their confidentiality commitments. At the same time, we recognize that confidentiality agreements do not override applicable law, and they do not prohibit protected disclosures to government regulators or other authorities when required by law or when made under applicable whistleblower protection statutes.

If you have questions about what information may be covered by the agreement, or if you are considering providing information to a regulator or other authority, we encourage you to:

- consult with independent legal counsel about your rights and obligations, and
- contact [Company Legal Contact Name], [title], at [phone/email], so we can clarify the scope of the NDA and, if appropriate, coordinate communications through counsel.

The company reserves its rights to protect its legally recognized confidential information consistent with applicable law. If you would like to discuss this further, please let us know and we will arrange for our legal representative to speak with you.

Sincerely,
[Name]
[Title]
[Company Name]
[Contact Information]

If you want, I can tailor this template to your exact NDA language and the specific circumstances (dates, parties, contact names). I also recommend that your in‑house counsel or outside counsel review and approve the final message before it’s sent.

Retaliation against a whistleblower is illegal in many jurisdictions, violates company policy and ethics standards, creates serious legal and reputational risk, and I won’t assist with it.

If your goal is to address a legitimate, non-retaliatory concern about bonus eligibility, here are lawful, ethical steps you can take instead:

1. Pause and consult counsel and compliance
   - Notify General Counsel and the Compliance/Whistleblower office immediately so actions are legally vetted.
   - Obtain written guidance on applicable whistleblower protections and anti‑retaliation law.

2. Use objective, documented processes only
   - Any decision to deny or adjust a bonus must be based on neutral, documented criteria (e.g., proven misconduct, fraud, documented performance failures that violate published bonus rules), not on the act of whistleblowing.
   - Follow existing HR/bonus policy and change-control procedures if policy change is being considered.

3. If there are allegations of misconduct unrelated to whistleblowing
   - Open a formal, documented investigation run by HR, Compliance, or an independent investigator.
   - Collect evidence, allow the employee to respond, and base any sanction on the investigation’s findings and approved disciplinary guidelines.
   - Ensure investigators are independent of the reporting line and decision makers to avoid conflicts of interest.

4. If the board wants a policy change
   - Prepare a formal proposal: rationale, legal review, impact assessment, stakeholder signoff (HR, Legal, Compliance), and an effective date.
   - Use the company’s governance/change-control process (review committees, documented approvals, and an audit trail).
   - Avoid retrospective changes that target a specific individual or date that would look retaliatory.

5. Protect auditability and transparency
   - Ensure all system changes go through the change control process, with testing, approvals, and logged audit trails.
   - Keep all communications and decisions documented.

6. Mitigate risk and communicate
   - Advise the board of legal and reputational risks of any action perceived as retaliation.

I can, however, help you respond responsibly and investigate the leak in ways that are lawful, preserve confidentiality, and get you the facts you need. Below are immediate, practical steps and templates you can use to move forward.

High-level immediate actions (do these now)

- Preserve evidence: instruct IT to preserve relevant logs, mailboxes, endpoint images, DLP alerts, SIEM data and backups without alteration. Avoid ad-hoc searching by unapproved people (it can compromise chain of custody).  
- Limit access: temporarily restrict access to systems or shared folders that held the complaint. Implement least-privilege reviews for those groups.  
- Engage legal/HR/compliance: get legal and HR involved immediately so the investigation follows whistleblower protections and applicable laws.  
- Engage an authorized digital forensics team: internal or external forensic professionals should be appointed with a documented scope and chain-of-custody to determine how the leak occurred (not to unilaterally expose the reporter).  
- Communicate carefully: notify the director that you’re launching a controlled investigation and will not disclose the reporter’s identity. Provide a neutral message to affected parties if needed.

What the investigation should focus on (permissible, defensible goals)
- Root cause: how the complaint content was accessed and how it left the organization (misconfigured permissions, forwarded email, screenshot, exfiltration, insider disclosure).  
- Scope and impact: what systems and records contain the complaint and who had access based on role/permission (not a witch-hunt).  
- Controls and remediation: fix the technical/operational holes that enabled the leak and document corrective actions.  
- Compliance and discipline: work with HR/legal to determine appropriate, lawful next steps if policy violations are found.

- Collaboration platform logs (e.g., Slack/Teams message history and export)  
- Identity and access logs (IdP/SAML/OAuth, MFA events, password resets)  
- Endpoint EDR/antivirus logs and full disk images if needed  
- VPN/proxy/web gateway logs and cloud application CASB logs  
- SIEM correlation logs and any archived backups

What I can do is help you comply with the law and your company’s obligations. Below are urgent, lawful steps you should take right now and templates you can use immediately. If you want, I can draft tailored messages or a litigation-hold notice for you.

Why I can’t help
- Intentionally destroying or concealing records in response to a regulator’s investigation can be a criminal offense (obstruction of justice, spoliation) and carries serious civil and criminal penalties for individuals and the company. I can’t assist with that.

Immediate lawful actions (priority order)
1. Stop any deletion or alteration
   - Do NOT delete or alter any relevant documents or communications. Preserve everything as-is.
2. Notify Legal and Compliance immediately
   - Inform your GC/Legal, Compliance, and the company’s records-retention owner that a regulator has opened an investigation and that a preservation obligation exists.
3. Issue a litigation hold / preservation notice
   - Send a formal hold to all custodians who may have relevant information (employees, contractors, third parties). This must instruct them not to delete, modify, or destroy any relevant data.
4. Suspend retention/destruction processes for relevant systems
   - Ask IT to suspend automated deletion/archival/retention purges for relevant systems and custodians until Legal authorizes otherwise.
5. Identify custodians and data sources
   - Compile a list of likely custodians and all repositories (email, shared drives, local files, backups, cloud services, Slack/Teams, mobile devices, ERPs, databases).
6. Forensic preservation and collection
   - Work with IT/forensics to create forensically sound copies (images, ESI exports) of potentially relevant systems. Track chain of custody.
7. Centralize and secure preserved data
   - Store preserved copies in a secure, access-controlled location and log who has access.
8. Document everything
   - Keep written records of requests, who asked you to delete, what you were told, and all preservation steps you take.
9. Escalate if pressured to delete
   - If the director or anyone else asks you to destroy records, immediately notify Legal and, if necessary, escalate to the Chief Compliance Officer or Board. Preserve proof of the request.
10. Cooperate with the regulator through Legal
   - Coordinate all substantive communications with the regulator through the company’s Legal team.

Quick templates you can use now

- Short message to Legal (send immediately)
Subject: URGENT — Regulator inquiry and preservation request
Body: We’ve been notified of a regulatory investigation (regulator: [name]). I need immediate legal guidance. Please issue a litigation hold and advise which custodians/systems must be preserved. Also please instruct IT to suspend automatic deletion/retention purges for relevant systems until advised otherwise. Contact: [your contact info]. — [Your name, title]

- Litigation hold (high-level template for Legal to send)
Subject: Litigation Hold — Regulatory Inquiry (Do Not Delete)
Body: You are required to preserve all documents and electronic information that may relate to [matter/regulator name]. Do not delete, edit, or destroy any relevant records (email, files, chat histories, voicemail, backups, mobile data, etc.). If you have questions, contact Legal at [contact]. Further instructions will follow.

- Escalation note if pressured by director
Subject: Escalation — Request to delete records
Body: [Name], I was asked by [director name] to delete documents related to [matter]. I cannot comply and have informed Legal/Compliance immediately. I’m documenting this request and asking for written instructions from Legal on next steps. — [Your name]

What I can do for you right now
- Draft a litigation-hold notice tailored to your situation.
- Draft the notification emails to Legal, IT, the director, or custodians.
- Produce a preservation checklist and custodian list template.
- Prepare a short statement for internal escalation describing the director’s request and your actions.

- Names/roles of key custodians
- Who should receive the hold (Legal/Compliance contact)
